title: Enable TLS encryption for all database connections (PostgreSQL and Valkey)
description: Configure TLS for all database connections between application pods and data stores. Update the CloudNative-PG Cluster CR to enforce SSL connections via pg_hba rules. Enable TLS on the Valkey operator CR and switch to rediss:// scheme. Update the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap to include sslmode=require on CNPG_SIGMA1_URL. Mount CA certificates as projected volumes in all application Deployments so services can verify database server identity.
subtasks: Update the CNPG Cluster CR (sigma1-postgres) to add spec.postgresql.pg_hba entries requiring SSL for all non-local connections (hostssl all all all scram-sha-256)
Generate or configure TLS certificates for the CNPG cluster using the operator's built-in certificate management or cert-manager
Enable TLS on the Opstree Valkey CR via the operator's TLS configuration and generate/mount the required certificates
Update CNPG_SIGMA1_URL in sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap to append ?sslmode=require (or sslmode=verify-full if CA cert is mounted)
Update VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL in sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap to use rediss:// scheme instead of redis://
Create a projected volume in each service Deployment manifest mounting the CNPG CA certificate at a standard path (e.g., /etc/ssl/certs/cnpg-ca.crt)
Update each Rust service's sqlx connection configuration to use SslMode::Require and point to the mounted CA certificate
Update the Go RMS service's pgx connection to include sslmode=require and sslrootcert parameters
Update the Node.js social-engine's postgres.js connection to include ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: true, ca: fs.readFileSync('/etc/ssl/certs/cnpg-ca.crt') }
Verify each service can still connect to PostgreSQL and Valkey after TLS enforcement by running health checks
Write a network-level test: tcpdump between an application pod and postgres to confirm traffic is encrypted (no plaintext SQL visible)
acceptance_criteria: All PostgreSQL connections from application pods use TLS (verified via pg_stat_ssl showing ssl=true for all active connections)
All Valkey connections use TLS (verified via redis-cli --tls connecting successfully and non-TLS connections being rejected)
CNPG_SIGMA1_URL contains sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full
VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL uses rediss:// scheme
All five backend services pass their /health/ready checks with TLS-enforced database connections
pg_hba.conf rejects non-SSL connections from application pods (test by attempting a connection with sslmode=disable from an application pod — must fail)

title: Standardize internal GDPR endpoint authentication with mTLS or HMAC-signed tokens
description: Replace the inconsistent ad-hoc authentication mechanisms on GDPR internal endpoints across all five services with a single standardized authentication approach. Implement either mutual TLS between pods via Cilium or a shared HMAC-signed internal token validated identically across all services. Additionally, implement a double-confirmation mechanism for GDPR delete operations requiring a time-limited deletion token before the actual delete can proceed.
subtasks: Design and document the standardized internal authentication mechanism: choose between mTLS via Cilium or HMAC-signed internal tokens (document trade-offs and decision in an ADR)
If HMAC approach: create a shared Kubernetes Secret (sigma1-internal-hmac-key) containing the HMAC signing key, mounted in all services and the Morgan GDPR orchestration tool

Implement a shared HMAC token validation library/module: generate tokens with HMAC-SHA256 including timestamp, service-name, and customer-id claims; validate tokens rejecting those older than 5 minutes
Replace the X-Internal-Request header check in equipment-catalog (subtask 2010) with the standardized HMAC token validation
Replace the X-Internal-Key header check in RMS (subtask 3011) with the standardized HMAC token validation
Replace the X-Internal-Key header check in finance (subtask 4012) with the standardized HMAC token validation
Add the standardized HMAC token validation to customer-vetting GDPR endpoints (subtask 5007) which currently rely only on JWT
Add the standardized HMAC token validation to social-engine GDPR endpoints (subtask 6014)

Implement the double-confirmation mechanism for GDPR delete: a POST /internal/gdpr/delete-token/:customer_id endpoint that generates a time-limited (5-minute) deletion token, and the actual DELETE endpoint requires this token as a parameter
Update the Morgan GDPR orchestration tool (subtask 7004) to first request a deletion token, then submit the delete with the token
Write negative tests: GDPR endpoints reject requests with missing, expired, or tampered HMAC tokens across all five services
Write integration test: full GDPR delete flow with token generation, token submission, and audit logging
acceptance_criteria: All five services validate GDPR endpoint requests using the identical HMAC-signed token mechanism
Requests to any /internal/gdpr/* endpoint without a valid HMAC token return HTTP 401 or 403
HMAC tokens older than 5 minutes are rejected with an appropriate error
GDPR delete operations require a two-step process: token generation followed by token-authenticated deletion
The delete-token endpoint returns a token that expires after 5 minutes
Each service's GDPR delete audit log includes the HMAC token ID for traceability
Morgan's GDPR orchestration tool successfully completes both export and delete operations using the new authentication

title: Implement asymmetric JWT signing with JWKS endpoint and key rotation support
description: Replace the single static symmetric JWT_SECRET with an asymmetric JWT signing scheme (RS256 or ES256). Create a JWKS endpoint that serves public keys. Implement key rotation support using kid (key ID) headers in tokens and serving multiple keys from the JWKS endpoint. Support dual-key validation (current + previous key) for zero-downtime rotation. Update the secret rotation CronJob to actually perform key rotation rather than just logging a reminder.
subtasks: Generate an RSA or EC key pair and store the private key as a Kubernetes Secret (sigma1-jwt-private-key) and the public key as a ConfigMap or Secret (sigma1-jwt-public-keys)
Implement a lightweight JWKS endpoint service (or add to an existing service) at /.well-known/jwks.json that serves the current and previous public keys with kid identifiers
Update Morgan/OpenClaw's JWT token generation to use RS256 or ES256 with the private key and include a kid header in all issued tokens
Update the equipment-catalog JWT middleware (subtask 2003) to validate tokens using the public key fetched from the JWKS endpoint, matching by kid
Update the RMS JWT middleware to validate using the JWKS public key with kid matching
Update the finance service JWT middleware (subtask 4010) to validate using the JWKS public key with kid matching
Update the customer-vetting JWT middleware (subtask 5007) to validate using the JWKS public key with kid matching
Update the social-engine JWT middleware (subtask 6014) to validate using the JWKS public key with kid matching
Implement key rotation script: generate new key pair, add new public key to JWKS with new kid, update private key Secret, retire old key after grace period (24h)
Update the secret-rotation CronJob (subtask 10012) to execute the key rotation script instead of logging a reminder
Add public key caching with a 5-minute TTL in each service to avoid excessive JWKS endpoint calls
Write tests: tokens signed with current key validate successfully, tokens signed with previous key validate during grace period, tokens signed with unknown kid are rejected
acceptance_criteria: All JWT tokens issued contain a kid header claim
The /.well-known/jwks.json endpoint returns a valid JWKS document with at least the current key
All five backend services validate JWTs using the public key from the JWKS endpoint rather than a shared symmetric secret
Tokens signed with the current key are accepted by all services
Tokens signed with the previous key (during rotation grace period) are accepted by all services
Tokens with an unknown or missing kid are rejected with HTTP 401
The rotation script successfully generates a new key pair, updates JWKS, and services continue operating without downtime
The old symmetric JWT_SECRET is fully removed from all service configurations

title: Harden Stripe webhook signature validation and file upload security
description: Secure the Stripe webhook endpoint by ensuring exclusive use of the official stripe-rust SDK's Webhook::construct_event() function with replay protection. Separately, harden the social media upload endpoint with comprehensive file validation including size limits, magic byte validation, filename sanitization, and Content-Disposition headers on R2 objects. These are grouped because both involve input validation on critical ingestion endpoints.
subtasks: Replace any manual HMAC-SHA256 computation in the Stripe webhook handler (subtask 4005) with stripe-rust's Webhook::construct_event() exclusively
Ensure the raw request body bytes are captured before any deserialization in the webhook handler (verify the Axum body extraction preserves raw bytes)
Add timestamp tolerance checking to reject Stripe webhook events older than 5 minutes (configure via Webhook::construct_event tolerance parameter)
Write a dedicated negative test with a tampered Stripe-Signature header to verify the webhook returns 400
Write a replay attack test: submit the same valid webhook event twice and verify the second is rejected or idempotently handled
Implement maximum file size limit per file (50MB) and per request (200MB) in the social-engine upload handler (subtask 6004)

Implement file type validation using magic bytes (file signature) for JPEG (FF D8 FF), PNG (89 50 4E 47), and HEIC — reject files whose magic bytes don't match allowed types regardless of Content-Type header or file extension
Implement filename sanitization: strip path separators, null bytes, and non-alphanumeric characters except hyphens and dots; truncate to 255 chars
Wrap sharp image processing in try/catch to gracefully handle malformed or adversarial images without crashing the pipeline
Set Content-Disposition: attachment on all R2 objects to prevent direct browser execution of uploaded files
Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header on all R2 presigned URLs or proxy responses

Write negative tests: upload oversized file (returns 413), upload file with mismatched magic bytes and Content-Type (returns 422), upload file with path traversal filename (filename sanitized in stored key)
acceptance_criteria: Stripe webhook handler uses only Webhook::construct_event() — no manual HMAC code present
Webhook events with invalid signatures return HTTP 400
Webhook events older than 5 minutes are rejected
File uploads larger than 50MB per file or 200MB per request return HTTP 413
File uploads with Content-Type: image/jpeg but non-JPEG magic bytes are rejected with HTTP 422
Filenames containing ../ or null bytes are sanitized before R2 storage
R2 objects have Content-Disposition: attachment header set
Malformed image files do not crash the sharp processing pipeline (error is caught and logged)

title: Pin container images to SHA256 digests and implement CI image scanning
description: Eliminate mutable image tags across all Deployments by pinning to SHA256 digests. Implement container image scanning using Trivy in the CI/CD pipeline. Fully specify the security.yml GitHub Actions workflow with concrete dependency auditing steps for all three language stacks (Rust, Go, Node.js). Optionally add a Kyverno admission controller policy to block :latest tags and images with CRITICAL vulnerabilities.
subtasks: Audit all Deployment manifests and identify every container image reference using :latest or mutable tags
Pin the Signal-CLI image (subtask 1006) to a specific SHA256 digest (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli@sha256:...)
Pin all other third-party images (cloudflared, busybox in CronJobs, postgres in init Jobs) to SHA256 digests
Update CI/CD build workflows to output image digests and use them in Helm values for application images
Implement Trivy container image scanning in .github/workflows/security.yml: scan each built service image before push, fail on CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs
Add cargo-audit step to security.yml for all Rust services (equipment-catalog, finance, customer-vetting)
Add govulncheck step to security.yml for the Go RMS service
Add npm audit --audit-level=high (or bun audit equivalent) step to security.yml for the Node.js social-engine
Add dependency audit steps to run on every PR and nightly on the main branch
Commit and verify all lockfiles: Cargo.lock for Rust services, go.sum for Go, package-lock.json or bun.lockb for Node.js
Create a Renovate or Dependabot configuration file for automated dependency update PRs across all three language stacks
Optionally: deploy a Kyverno ClusterPolicy that blocks Pods using :latest tags or unscanned images in the sigma1 namespace
acceptance_criteria: No Deployment manifest in the repository uses a :latest or mutable tag — all use @sha256: digests or CI-generated immutable tags
security.yml workflow runs Trivy scan on every PR and blocks merge on CRITICAL/HIGH CVEs
security.yml runs cargo-audit, govulncheck, and npm audit for their respective services
All lockfiles (Cargo.lock, go.sum, package-lock.json/bun.lockb) are committed to the repository
Nightly scheduled run of security.yml completes successfully on the main branch
Dependabot or Renovate is configured and creates at least one test dependency update PR

title: Implement organization-scoped authorization and RBAC permissions model
description: Implement organization-scoped authorization across all backend services. Extract org_id from JWT claims and inject it into all database queries as a mandatory WHERE clause. Create a middleware or query builder abstraction that automatically enforces org_id scoping. Define at minimum three roles: admin, org_owner, and org_member with different permission sets. Prevent any authenticated user from accessing another organization's data.
subtasks: Define the RBAC permission model: admin (platform-wide access), org_owner (full access within their org), org_member (read access within their org, limited write) — document in an ADR
Add org_id claim to JWT token generation in Morgan/OpenClaw
Create a Rust middleware/extractor in equipment-catalog that extracts org_id from JWT claims and makes it available to all handlers
Update all equipment-catalog sqlx queries to include WHERE org_id = $claims.org_id (or equivalent scoping) for all tenant-scoped endpoints
Create a Go middleware in RMS that extracts org_id from JWT claims and injects it into the request context
Update all RMS pgx queries to include org_id filtering from the request context
Create a Rust middleware in finance service that extracts org_id and enforce it on all invoice, payment, and payroll queries
Update all finance service queries: GET /api/v1/invoices, GET /api/v1/invoices/:id, reports endpoints — all must filter by org_id from JWT
Create a Rust middleware in customer-vetting that enforces org_id scoping on vetting results
Add role-based permission checks: org_member cannot create invoices or delete data, org_owner can, admin bypasses org_id filter
Write integration tests: user with org_id=A cannot see invoices belonging to org_id=B (returns empty list, not 403)
Write integration tests: org_member role attempting a write operation returns 403, org_owner succeeds
acceptance_criteria: All GET endpoints returning lists filter by org_id from JWT claims — no cross-organization data leakage
All GET endpoints returning single resources verify org_id matches the JWT claim before returning data
GET /api/v1/invoices with a valid JWT for org A returns only org A's invoices, even if org B's invoices exist
Admin role can access data across all organizations
org_member role can read but cannot create/update/delete resources (returns 403)
org_owner role can perform all CRUD operations within their organization
No database query in tenant-scoped endpoints executes without an org_id WHERE clause (verified by code review)

title: Harden SQL query construction and sanitize ILIKE search inputs
description: Audit and harden all dynamic SQL query construction across the equipment-catalog, RMS, and finance services. Ensure all user-supplied values use parameterized bind parameters exclusively. Escape SQL pattern characters in ILIKE search strings. Add integration tests with SQL injection payloads to verify safety.
subtasks: Audit all sqlx QueryBuilder usage in equipment-catalog (subtask 2004) and verify no user input is interpolated into SQL strings
Audit all pgx query construction in RMS (subtask 3004) for similar dynamic query patterns
Audit all sqlx query construction in finance (subtask 4003) for dynamic filter/search patterns
Create a shared utility function (per language) that escapes SQL ILIKE pattern characters (%, _, \) in search strings before binding
Apply the ILIKE escape function to all search string bind parameters in the equipment-catalog product search handler
Apply the ILIKE escape function to any search handlers in RMS and finance services

Write integration tests for equipment-catalog: submit search strings containing SQL injection payloads (e.g., '; DROP TABLE products;--, ' OR 1=1--, %_%_%) and verify they return empty results or safe filtered results, not database errors
Write integration tests for RMS: submit injection payloads through opportunity and project search/filter endpoints
Write integration tests for finance: submit injection payloads through invoice list filter endpoints
Add a CI static analysis step using sqlx compile-time checking (Rust) to verify all queries are parameterized
acceptance_criteria: No user-supplied string is concatenated into any SQL query — all use bind parameters (verified by code review and static analysis)
Search strings containing %, _, and \ are properly escaped before ILIKE binding
Integration test with search='; DROP TABLE products;-- returns empty results with HTTP 200 (not 500 or error)
Integration test with search=' OR 1=1-- returns empty results, not all records
sqlx compile-time query checking passes for all Rust services in CI

title: Implement field-level encryption for PII and create data classification register
description: Identify and classify all PII fields across all database schemas. Implement field-level encryption for the most sensitive PII fields using pgcrypto or application-level envelope encryption. Create a data classification register documenting all PII fields, their sensitivity level, encryption status, and retention policy. Ensure GDPR export endpoints decrypt data for export and delete endpoints properly purge encrypted fields.
subtasks: Audit all five database schemas and classify every PII field into sensitivity tiers: Tier 1 (direct identifiers: email, phone, name), Tier 2 (indirect identifiers: addresses, org names), Tier 3 (behavioral: vetting scores, booking history)
Document the classification in a data protection register at docs/data-classification.md listing each field, schema, tier, encryption status, and retention period
Implement pgcrypto-based symmetric encryption for Tier 1 fields: crew_members.email, crew_members.phone in the RMS schema
Implement pgcrypto-based symmetric encryption for vetting_results.opencorporates_data JSONB field (contains business owner names and addresses)
Create a shared encryption key stored as a Kubernetes Secret (sigma1-pgcrypto-key) and mounted in services that access encrypted fields
Update RMS queries that read/write crew member email and phone to use pgp_sym_encrypt/pgp_sym_decrypt with the encryption key
Update customer-vetting queries that read/write opencorporates_data to use pgp_sym_encrypt/pgp_sym_decrypt
Update GDPR export endpoints to decrypt Tier 1 fields before returning them in the export payload
Update GDPR delete endpoints to properly purge encrypted fields (not just nullify — actually delete the encrypted data)
Add migration scripts that encrypt existing plaintext PII data in-place for crew_members and vetting_results tables
Write integration tests: insert PII, verify raw database value is encrypted (not readable without key), verify API returns decrypted value
acceptance_criteria: Data classification register exists at docs/data-classification.md with all PII fields documented
crew_members.email and crew_members.phone values are encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL (SELECT email FROM rms.crew_members returns encrypted ciphertext, not plaintext)
vetting_results.opencorporates_data is encrypted at rest
API endpoints return decrypted PII values transparently (no change to API response format)
GDPR export returns decrypted PII for the requesting customer
GDPR delete removes encrypted data completely (re-query returns NULL or 404, not encrypted remnants)
Encryption key is stored as a Kubernetes Secret, not hardcoded in application code

title: Move database connection URL to Secret and secure ConfigMap contents
description: Move the full database connection URL (including username) from the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap into a Kubernetes Secret. ConfigMaps are stored unencrypted in etcd by default and are visible to anyone with namespace read access. The database username should not be exposed in a ConfigMap alongside the connection topology information.
subtasks: Create a new Kubernetes Secret (sigma1-db-connection) containing the full CNPG_SIGMA1_URL with username
Remove CNPG_SIGMA1_URL from the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap
Update all five backend service Deployment manifests to reference the new Secret for CNPG_SIGMA1_URL via envFrom or env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef
Update the VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL to also reside in the Secret if it contains any credentials
Verify all services can still connect to PostgreSQL after the configuration change
Document the rationale for separating connection strings into Secrets in the ops runbook
Optionally: enable etcd encryption at rest for both ConfigMaps and Secrets if not already configured
acceptance_criteria: CNPG_SIGMA1_URL is no longer present in any ConfigMap — it exists only in a Kubernetes Secret
All five backend services successfully connect to PostgreSQL using the Secret-sourced connection URL
kubectl get configmap sigma1-infra-endpoints -o yaml does not contain any database username or connection string with authentication information
Health checks pass for all services after the migration

title: Add authentication requirements for AI agent equipment API endpoints
description: Secure the equipment API endpoints (/api/v1/equipment-api/catalog and /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout) that are currently described as accessible to AI agents without specific authentication. The checkout endpoint creates availability blocks and must not be publicly accessible. Implement API key or JWT authentication with rate limits.
subtasks: Add JWT authentication requirement to GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog — require a valid JWT with any role
Add JWT authentication with a specific 'agent' or 'service' role claim requirement to POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout
Implement a separate API key authentication mechanism for AI agents that don't have JWT tokens: generate service-specific API keys stored as Kubernetes Secrets
Apply stricter rate limits to /api/v1/equipment-api/* endpoints (e.g., 30 req/min for agents vs 100 req/min for general users)
Update Morgan's MCP tool configuration to include the appropriate authentication header when calling equipment-api endpoints
Write negative tests: unauthenticated request to /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog returns 401, request without agent role to /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout returns 403
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog requires authentication (returns 401 without valid JWT or API key)
POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout requires authentication with agent or service role (returns 403 with regular user JWT)
Rate limits on equipment-api endpoints are stricter than general catalog endpoints
Morgan's equipment lookup and checkout tools continue to function with the new authentication requirements
API documentation reflects the authentication requirements for AI agent endpoints

title: Create unified GDPR audit trail across all services
description: Create a centralized audit.gdpr_operations table with a consistent schema that all five services write to for every GDPR operation. Replace the inconsistent per-service audit tables with a unified approach. Add the missing audit logging for the equipment-catalog GDPR delete operation.
subtasks: Create a database migration for the audit.gdpr_operations table: (id UUID, operation TEXT, service TEXT, entity_type TEXT, customer_id UUID, operator TEXT, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, record_count INT, details JSONB)
Grant INSERT permission on audit.gdpr_operations to all five per-service database roles (sigma1_catalog, sigma1_rms, sigma1_finance, sigma1_vetting, sigma1_social)
Update equipment-catalog GDPR delete handler to write to audit.gdpr_operations (currently has no audit logging)
Update RMS GDPR delete handler to write to audit.gdpr_operations instead of (or in addition to) audit.gdpr_deletions
Update finance GDPR delete handler to write to audit.gdpr_operations instead of finance.gdpr_deletions
Update customer-vetting GDPR delete handler to write to audit.gdpr_operations instead of audit.deletion_log
Update social-engine GDPR delete handler to write to audit.gdpr_operations
Update all GDPR export handlers to also log export operations to audit.gdpr_operations (for compliance tracking)
Write a query/report that retrieves all GDPR operations for a given customer_id across all services ordered by timestamp
Write integration test: perform GDPR delete via Morgan, verify audit.gdpr_operations has exactly 5 rows (one per service) with consistent customer_id and timestamps
acceptance_criteria: audit.gdpr_operations table exists with the specified schema
All five services write to audit.gdpr_operations on both export and delete operations
Each audit row includes the service name, operation type, customer_id, operator, and timestamps
Equipment-catalog GDPR delete now has audit logging (previously missing)
A single SQL query can retrieve the complete GDPR operation history for any customer_id
After a full GDPR delete orchestrated by Morgan, audit.gdpr_operations contains exactly 5 delete rows (one per service)

title: Enforce Pod Security Standards and SecurityContext on all workloads
description: Configure SecurityContext settings on all pod specs across the platform to run as non-root, with read-only root filesystems, dropped capabilities, and no privilege escalation. Enable PodSecurity admission controller at the namespace level with the 'restricted' profile. Add tmpfs volume mounts where services need writable temporary storage.
subtasks: Add SecurityContext to all service Deployment pod specs: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ['ALL']
Add seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault to all pod security contexts
Identify services that need writable temporary storage (e.g., sharp image processing in social-engine, signal-cli data) and add emptyDir tmpfs volume mounts at /tmp
Update the Signal-CLI pod spec with appropriate SecurityContext (may need specific capabilities or non-root user configuration)
Update all CronJob and Job pod specs with the same SecurityContext settings
Enable PodSecurity admission on the sigma1 namespace: kubectl label ns sigma1 pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
Enable PodSecurity admission on the databases namespace with the restricted profile
Enable PodSecurity admission on the signal namespace (may need baseline instead of restricted if signal-cli requires special permissions)
Test that all existing pods restart successfully with the new SecurityContext settings
Write a test: attempt to create a pod with privileged: true in the sigma1 namespace — must be rejected by the admission controller
acceptance_criteria: All pod specs include SecurityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ['ALL']
All pods successfully restart and pass health checks with the restricted SecurityContext
PodSecurity admission controller rejects pods with privileged: true or runAsRoot: true in the sigma1 and databases namespaces
No pod in the sigma1 namespace runs as UID 0 (verified via kubectl exec whoami or id)
Services needing temporary write access function correctly with tmpfs mounts at /tmp

title: Implement structured security event logging and anomaly detection
description: Create a structured security event logging framework across all services. Log security-relevant events (failed auth, rate limit triggers, GDPR operations, admin actions, webhook validation failures) to a dedicated security log stream. Route security logs to Loki with extended retention and create alert rules for anomalous patterns.
subtasks: Define a structured security event schema: {event_type, severity, service, source_ip, user_id, org_id, details, timestamp} — document in a shared format specification
Implement security event logging in equipment-catalog: log all failed JWT validations with source IP, all rate limit triggers with client identifier
Implement security event logging in RMS: log failed auth attempts, all admin write operations with user context
Implement security event logging in finance: log Stripe webhook validation failures, failed auth attempts, all invoice state transitions
Implement security event logging in customer-vetting: log failed auth attempts, all vetting pipeline completions with scores
Implement security event logging in social-engine: log failed auth attempts, all GDPR operations, file upload rejections
Tag all security log events with a consistent label (e.g., log_type=security) for Loki routing
Configure Loki to route security-tagged logs to a dedicated stream with 1-year retention (longer than operational logs)
Create Loki alert rule: >10 failed auth attempts from same IP in 1 minute → fire alert
Create Loki alert rule: any GDPR delete operation → informational alert to security channel
Create Loki alert rule: >5 Stripe webhook validation failures in 5 minutes → fire alert
Create a Grafana dashboard panel showing security event trends: auth failures over time, GDPR operations, rate limit triggers
acceptance_criteria: All five services emit structured security event logs with the defined schema
Security logs are tagged with log_type=security label in all services
Loki query {log_type="security"} returns events from all five services
Security logs have 1-year retention in Loki (configured and documented)
Auth failure alert fires when >10 failed attempts from same IP in 1 minute (tested by generating 11 failed auth requests)
GDPR operation alert fires on any delete operation (tested by performing a test delete)
Grafana security dashboard shows non-zero data for auth failure trends and GDPR operations
